did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep hybrid framework for skin lesion detection and classification. Specifically, it employs Selective Kernel U-Net (SK-UNet) for segmentation, a hybrid 1D-CNN–GRU model for classification, and the Fossa Optimization Algorithm (FOA) for feature selection on dermoscopic images from the ISIC and DermMNIST datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel 'optimal deep hybrid framework' involving specific components (SK-UNet, FOA, 1D-CNN-GRU) and reports original experimental results, including classification accuracies of 97.6% and 95.6% on ISIC and DermMNIST datasets. Although it contains a literature review section, its primary purpose is presenting new algorithmic development and validation, not summarizing prior literature.

title: An Optimal Deep Hybrid Framework with Selective Kernel U-Net for Skin Lesion Detection and Classification
brief_summary: This study proposes an optimal deep hybrid framework for automated skin lesion detection and classification to address challenges such as image noise and lesion variability. The methodology integrates Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization and Wiener filtering for preprocessing, Selective Kernel U-Net for segmentation, and the Fossa Optimization Algorithm for feature selection. A hybrid 1D-CNN–GRU classifier is employed to learn spatial and sequential patterns from the extracted features. The framework was evaluated on the ISIC and DermMNIST benchmark datasets using standard performance metrics. Experimental results demonstrated high classification accuracies of 97.6% on the ISIC dataset and 95.6% on the DermMNIST dataset. The findings confirm that the proposed hybrid approach offers reliable and scalable potential for assisting clinical decision-making in early skin cancer diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Nevus, Vascular Lesion, Actinic Keratosis, Pigmented Basal Cell Carcinoma, Seborrheic Keratosis).
all_task_types.details: The paper employs a hybrid 1D-CNN–GRU classifier with softmax activation to perform multi-class classification of skin lesions. The primary objective is accurate diagnosis and detection of specific lesion types. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy (97.6% on ISIC, 95.6% on DermMNIST), precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC. The task involves distinguishing between multiple discrete classes rather than predicting continuous values.

The framework utilizes Selective Kernel U-Net (SK-UNet) for precise lesion segmentation to isolate lesion areas from the background. This is a preprocessing step for feature extraction and subsequent classification, not the primary diagnostic output task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A widely used benchmark dermoscopic image dataset for skin lesion analysis and melanoma detection, providing high-quality images with annotated lesion regions across multiple classes. It is publicly available via Kaggle.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid 1D-CNN–GRU classifier and optimizing features with FOA. The dataset was split into 70% for training.
use.validation: Used for validation. The dataset was split into 15% for validation.
use.testing: Used for testing and performance evaluation. The dataset was split into 15% for testing. A confusion matrix analysis noted 199 test instances.

datasets_used.name: DermMNIST Dataset
datasets_used.description: A standardized benchmark dataset offering annotated dermoscopic images for lesion classification tasks, used to evaluate model generalizability. It is publicly available via Kaggle.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid 1D-CNN–GRU classifier and optimizing features with FOA. The dataset was split into 70% for training.
use.validation: Used for validation. The dataset was split into 15% for validation.
use.testing: Used for testing and performance evaluation. The dataset was split into 15% for testing. A confusion matrix analysis noted 49 test instances.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC) and compared qualitatively to existing methods.

architecture_used.main_model: Optimal Deep Hybrid Framework (Selective Kernel U-Net for segmentation, Fossa Optimization Algorithm for feature selection, Hybrid 1D-CNN–GRU for classification)

classification_architecture_layers: GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit with update gate, reset gate, candidate hidden state)

classification_or_output_head.description: Fully connected layer converting recovered features into resulting classes, transformed into probability using softmax function

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for contrast enhancement
conclusions: The proposed optimal deep hybrid framework, integrating CLAHE/Wiener preprocessing, Selective Kernel U-Net (SK-UNet) segmentation, Fossa Optimization Algorithm (FOA) feature selection, and a hybrid 1D-CNN–GRU classifier, achieves high classification accuracy of 97.6% on the ISIC dataset and 95.6% on the DermMNIST dataset.

The framework outperforms existing feature selection methods (PCA, LASSO, GA, PSO) and other deep learning models in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC across both benchmark datasets.
The hybrid 1D-CNN–GRU classifier effectively learns both spatial and sequential feature patterns, resulting in robust multi-class classification performance with minimal error rates.

The Fossa Optimization Algorithm (FOA) successfully reduces feature redundancy and enhances classifier efficiency, with color and texture features identified as having the highest importance for classification.
The model demonstrates strong generalization capabilities, evidenced by steady convergence and minimal overfitting during training (30 epochs) on both datasets.
The framework shows potential for assisting clinical decision-making and early skin cancer detection by providing a reliable, accurate, and scalable diagnostic tool.

While the model performs well overall, challenges remain in distinguishing certain lesion types with high similarity (e.g., NV, MEL, BKL), as indicated by t-SNE/PCA visualizations and specific misclassifications in the confusion matrices.
important_remarks: The study lacks clinical validation; results are limited to experimental performance on benchmark datasets (ISIC and DermMNIST) and may not reflect real-world clinical variability.
The framework relies on publicly available datasets, which may introduce population or dataset bias and limits generalizability to actual clinical scenarios.
Computational complexity of the deep learning and optimization components may restrict real-time deployment on resource-constrained systems.
No explicit robustness evaluation was performed; future work is needed to assess performance under controlled noise, illumination variations, and lesion morphology perturbations.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the significance of performance differences.
Specific misclassifications were noted between visually similar lesion types (e.g., NV, MEL, BKL), indicating challenges in distinguishing classes with high feature similarity.

The model was trained and tested on a fixed 70/15/15 split without mention of cross-validation or external independent test sets, raising potential concerns about data leakage or overfitting to specific splits.
The funding source (APVV-21-0502) relates to intracranial aneurysms, which is unrelated to the dermatological focus of the study, potentially indicating a mismatch in funding alignment.
